speaker: interviewer
text: An interview with Simon Stemer, on November 13, 1995. You were telling me about the beginning of the ghetto.

speaker: interviewee
text: When they said all the people, they don't go back to work. They have to do special work for the government, in the ghetto. This mean they have to dig the holes. They give them a blueprint, and everything. It's when they said this I said, I will not go dig a hole for myself. Because I know the holes is for the people, for the Jews. The other people went. I don't go. Only one day, I was thinking I will go out, see how this is. Was just 6 kilometer into town. I went out. And I saw how this is. They dig the holes-- big holes, smalls holes. I ask whether you need-- the big holes, I know know they need. They need small holes for the wash rooms. The [INAUDIBLE] the wash rooms. They dig small holes too. This was one day. I just don't touch the shovel. I don't do nothing. I just saw it, and I come back. And then I said to the people-- not everybody has thinking to get some arrangement, a Gentile, to hide. And some people had to make some meantime hiding places where you live. It was taking-- they was working over there about two weeks. And the people one day, when they come back with the shovels, they said leave the shovels outside from the gate, from the ghetto. Oh, as we know, it is a decision the the last minute. I have a friend in my town. When I come back from Kostopol, I told him the story. I thought maybe we will go in the woods. Maybe we will do something. He worked in a good place. And he said, no, will nothing happen. In the night, when this was already, he come over. And he said, [NON-ENGLISH]. I said, well, we will go. It is all over already police, with Gestapo. They bring [INAUDIBLE] police from [NON-ENGLISH], [NON-ENGLISH] from Poland. Oh, they got 15,000 people in [INAUDIBLE]. They have maybe more. It's no place to-- 6 o'clock in the morning-- this was 17 days [INAUDIBLE]. [PAUSES FOR 11 SECONDS] We saw, in the morning, 6 o'clock, the ghetto is all around already police. And they to shoot, just to scare the people not to run. My brother make a little hiding place for two people, in this room, for him and his wife. Only the wife have two little kids. She don't go in. I was hiding in a place, with another man and two ladies. We make a hiding place in a chimney. They have a big chimney over there. And we make, from the attic, to go in the chimney. And over there, we was hiding. We was not having what to eat, drink-- nothing. We was hiding over there about two days. We got there on a Thursday. Over there was going [NON-ENGLISH. They called it a [NON-ENGLISH]. If you take over there a little bit water, you saw the bugs running in the water. Only we don't have other water. We took this little water. The [NON-ENGLISH] said this was not vegetables. He said before everybody should have a little place to make a yard, to grow some vegetables. He said [INAUDIBLE] was over there places carrots, with onions, with cucumbers. From this, we live-- and the little water from the [INAUDIBLE], from this we live 14 days-- two weeks. This was not too far from over there was a pump, which [INAUDIBLE] pumps special. The whole town was taking it over there. As I just was hoping to get out, when I get out, if I live, to be able to go to this pump, to get a drink the water. After two weeks being in this, we hear Jewish [INAUDIBLE] going around. They talk Jewish-- will nothing more happen to this town. In Jewish-- [NON-ENGLISH]. [NON-ENGLISH]-- no more killing. We come out [INAUDIBLE] over there a guy by the [? Shia ?] [? Lablanda. ?] The [NON-ENGLISH] have some people. When they took the clothes, the people went naked to the graves. Everybody was naked. The people-- he had some Jewish people for they work for their clothes-- was a school over there. They call this the red school. And they took over there the clothes. I just was before there-- I was going to work. I was wishing, everyday, I wish I get killed, and not to know when, not to come back to the ghetto. Because I know what is waiting for me in the ghetto. After the 14 days, people start to come together. People was hiding in the woods. People was hiding in different places. But Gentile came together. There was-- they got together about 2,000, 3,000 people. It was taking about two months, til-- til November. And the [NON-ENGLISH] said they make a new [NON-ENGLISH], a new Jewish committee, a Judenrat. And the people going back to work. And nothing happened to this. Only here was about 15,000, 16,000 people. Before now, it's just 2,000, 3,000. You have to make a small ghetto. We make a small ghetto. We was over there about six months, until May. I was going to work. In this time, I went with my brother to work in a factory, they make belts. We were making the belts. We were over there six Jews, and one Russian manager. When you go out from the ghetto, you took out a [INAUDIBLE], or something-- a pair of pants. You get food, everything. Everyday, somebody else brings something. And we went out over there. We was out of the ghetto. And we cooked over there. I was over there the cook. And we cooked until we'd have lunch. As I make everything ready, the manager [INAUDIBLE] lunch. And after lunch, I was working already. We worked over there. Later, the [NON-ENGLISH] said he will give special papers for the people who they have trades-- carpenters, tailors, shoemakers. He give out maybe about, 200, 300 this special certificate. And these people will have a separate place to live, a little ghetto. 600 people was over there. And we was in this one. The people was scared every time will be-- somebody said tomorrow will be a [NON-ENGLISH]. People was running out. Everybody have a place to hide by a Gentile. It was in this Judenrat-- when I work in this Judenrat was the president, Mr. [? Kulich. ?] I don't have time to go to take in the middle of a haircut. Sunday, we work in the ghetto, to make belts. We work in the ghetto. As I went in the work, I went to take a haircut. And this [? Kulich ?] comes in in the place, where I was working over there. And I was not in.

speaker: interviewer
text: They ask, where's [? Simon? ?] Said, he went to the haircut-- take a haircut. He come in to the barber. I was sitting in a chair. He hit me. I fail down. You bandit. You want to kill us? You go back away from the work? If I work, he will live. If I will not work, he will get killed. What can you do? In the middle of haircut-- I went back to work. People was always running out from the ghetto. And later, they hear it's quiet. They come back to the ghetto-- to the little ghetto. Later, they liquidated the third ghetto.

speaker: interviewee
text: The second ghetto was liquidated Friday morning. Nobody can go no place. And nobody-- it was already around Ukrainian police. And they start to shoot in the morning. They killed some people they want to run out, they killed them. Was a water [INAUDIBLE]. Some people were swimming. They shot the people in the water. This was the second ghetto that was liquidated. This took about two days. And we have over there, where we live, a hiding place. Was over there, maybe three yards by a yard, was 18 people just standing, two days. Nobody have nothing-- no water. One woman was so thirsty she took urine, to kill her thirst. I have two pieces bread I grabbed in the pocket. Was already mushy-- I ate this. Later, we hear some Jewish people talking. I went up, take a look who's over there. They was taking together this merchandise where the people lived. And they bring it to the red school. Was over there a friend of mind, [? Gabols. ?] I ask [? Gabols ?] help me. He told me, meet 12 o'clock here. I will take you. Meantime, little by little, he all out. A couple days, he took all out the other ghetto. He took me with my-- my brother was alive still. He took me with my brother. Over there, in this little ghetto, they don't allow you to be over there. Of course, they just allowed the people what they have the [NON-ENGLISH]. Only in this little ghetto, we was hiding. We was sleeping in a shed. Rain, got wet-- only we stayed over there a little while. Over there, in this Judenrat, they have one educated man in Hebrew, a high scholar man. He called-- the one Sunday, he called all the people from this. And he told us, if you running out from the ghetto, we endanger us. He told two stories. If man is riding in a boat on the ocean, and he have a nail, and make it with the nail a little hole, this little hole the water is coming in. And everybody is cry [INAUDIBLE]. It's the same thing. If you run out one, you can spoil-- they can kill the all 600, 700 people over there. Later he said another story. I will not forget the stories. When [NON-ENGLISH] make the [NON-ENGLISH], his God told him to bring in all the animals, two in the [NON-ENGLISH]. And the question-- the wild animals-- how come they don't eat up the animals for they was going in a [NON-ENGLISH]. The answer is, he said, if they have [INAUDIBLE] them, they don't have in the mind the other one. They will save his life, he don't remember the other thing. Same thing with the Germans. The Germans-- they not getting very bad [INAUDIBLE], going back from Stalingrad. This was already in the winter time-- September, October. It was winter over there. This was in this time. Is they have to do it themselves, and they will forget on us. In another [INAUDIBLE] the [? Kulich ?], when the Jewish people-- yeah, the [NON-ENGLISH] said, all the [INAUDIBLE] was the Jewish people was living have be [INAUDIBLE], have to be cleaned up. Over there was a rich man. He got a briefcase, with gold-- five ruble gold, 10 ruble gold-- the Russian gold pieces. The people [INAUDIBLE] took apart, the people where they was. And the second ghetto worked in this, to take apart-- some people went outside. Not all the people. Is where they find gold, or something, that give to this [? Kulich, ?] to this guy, the president. [? Kulich ?] had the vice president-- [NON-ENGLISH]. He lived good with this-- [? Kulich ?] lived [INAUDIBLE]. And he give him a lot of money. [? Kulich ?] give a lot of money [INAUDIBLE]. And [? Kulich ?] said, this guy, the vice president, he told him-- the [NON-ENGLISH]-- he told him, if this will get something happen, he will tell him. And this will be the time to run out from the ghetto. Meantime, when [INAUDIBLE], they stand around the ghetto, is the [INAUDIBLE], the [INAUDIBLE] they throw into [? Kulich's ?] house. And me, and my brother, and another five people was hiding in this room. This was Friday, until Saturday night. Everybody got a place, a Gentile, to be keep [INAUDIBLE]. Nobody was thinking it will take so long. Only because the Russian coming so fast-- we was [INAUDIBLE]. Everybody was thinking he will go out, and he will go to the Gentile, and he will-- over there, we hide it, and he will [INAUDIBLE]. Me and my brother, we have a place. This was Saturday night. We walked out. I walked out. This was December 15-- December 13, 1943 was the last ghetto liquidated. This was December 15. We walked out. I fall down. My brother comes and pick me up. I go a little bit, and I fall down again. My brother left. And I was laying. I was thinking, any minute some policeman will come, will take me, and he will shot me. Cannot go. I was laying here outside maybe an hour, or two hours. Nobody comes. And nobody [INAUDIBLE]. I went back. I find, in a house, a little bit toast. And I have a half a kettle water, with a little glass. And I went in this bunker. And I was staying over there. The bunker was under soil. Mice was running around. I ate every day a toast, with a little bit water. This kept me until January 2, 1944. If I don't have [INAUDIBLE] to the food, I have to go out. I go out. I want to go north. I come out south. Because my head was not-- after laying 20 days in a bunker. And I come to [INAUDIBLE] water. And I start to break [INAUDIBLE] back. I was running, going out the whole night. And I was on [INAUDIBLE]. I was walking. Later, I come to a place. It was already probably about 4 or 5 o'clock. I saw a little light. And I know this place is nuns. The nuns' place over there. I sit down [INAUDIBLE] stop. And I remind myself, not far from over there was a guy attacked [? in Champagne. ?] He was a Gentile. And he want me to call me to come with him, to stay. And he will keep us until the war will be over. Because he don't have [INAUDIBLE] to pay the doctor, the Gentile. And I have a little bit money. When I work in the mill, I took out some flowers. And I sold. And I brought the gold pieces, little gold pieces. And he know [INAUDIBLE] the doctor told me to come to see about this guy, [INAUDIBLE] the place. Yes, I feel [INAUDIBLE] with my brother will be for us better to go over there. This was in the city. And the other one was out of the city, as I was thinking. It was not too far from the nuns to this [? Gochinski ?], what he lived. I come over there. Impossible to-- it was so [INAUDIBLE], and nobody can come in. Because it was the war time, they was get [INAUDIBLE]. There was a shed in the side. I go in, in the shed. And I hear the pig. If I hear the pig, because I know somebody will come to feed the pig. [INAUDIBLE] in the morning comes in this guy [? Gochinski, ?] Leon [? Gochinski ?]. I have a bunch of the Ukraine money. And I fall to his legs. And I beg him, Mr. [? Gochiski, ?] take me in, in this room. He said, [NON-ENGLISH], wait. I wait. He went up to the house. He brings me down a little tea, with a quarter of a bread. I ate it up. And I fall to sleep. I sleep in the straw until the night. In the night, he wake me up.

speaker: interviewer
text: He bring me supper. Leon take me in-- [NON-ENGLISH] [? Gochinski ?] take me in-- wait, wait. No? I ate the supper. I fall asleep. In the morning, he brings me again breakfast-- tea and bread. I ate up. And I fall to sleep again. In the night, he comes in. And he said [? Chia, ?] come.

speaker: interviewee
text: I come with him. He took me up in the house. They give me water, with a [NON-ENGLISH] they call. I don't know how this is-- a big place you can wash. I wash up, and take me down in this room. In this room over there was already four grown ups, and three children, and I was already eight people. OK, I have, in this time, I have the gold pieces. I paid to this guy [? Gochinski ?] 10 [NON-ENGLISH]-- a $10 gold piece for [INAUDIBLE] to keep. All right, he give me. Later-- and we have over there, digged out, in this hole, where we was hiding. In one side was a wash room. You used the wash room. And in the other side was a little hole, to get out little bit water. We was all digging. They was digging. This guy, the doctor, he was in love with this [? Gochinski's ?] wife. He went, every night. About 10 o'clock, we went up over there. I don't know what he did, but [INAUDIBLE] he come back. One day, in the morning, Mr. [? Gochinski ?] come down. And he said to the doctor, [NON-ENGLISH]. The wife was called [? Zosha. ?] And he tell him, you will not have-- you will not get nothing. And I will not have nothing. If she passed away, you will not have nothing from her. And I will not have nothing.

speaker: interviewer
text: The doctor start to cry. There was over there a woman what she was hiding. [? Ruhala ?] was her name. She asked him, doctor, why are you crying? She said, [? Zosha ?] was to me better like my mother. We were sitting-- this first in January.

speaker: interviewee
text: This guy [? Gochinski ?] always bring the news. The Russian are coming. The Russian are coming. The Russian are coming. As [INAUDIBLE] March, they come to [INAUDIBLE]. And over there, they stop. This was 50 miles-- 58 kilometers from [? Vladimir. ?] And they was using [INAUDIBLE] ammunition. They was failing to bring new ammunition. It was taking until late April. From the beginning of May, they start to move in, to go, to come to Ludmir to go, or Poland. This was Ukraine.
